entries.title: Morals on the Book of Job, Book XXIV on job 34:5
entries.excerpt:
For Job hath said, I am just, and God hath subverted my judgment. For in judging me there is falsehood: and mine arrow is violent without any sin.

He complains that Job had spoken these things, [See chap. 27, 2] which the words of the sacred history prove on examination that he had never said. But he, who had sought for a judgment on equal terms, proceeds to promulgate a sentence from a fault of his own invention.

entries.title: Commentary on Job on job 34:5
entries.excerpt: Therefore, he proposes the argument of Job saying them, "since you have said, Job: I am a just man." He had said this already, "I will not desert my justification which I have begun to have." (27:6) Further he had shown his justice clearly in many things above in Chapter Thirty One. Eliud continues, "and God is overturning my cause." Eliud takes this to be the same as what Job had said in Chapter Twenty Seven, "Long live God who has rejected my cause," (v.2) and the same seems to pertain to what he had said in Chapter Nineteen, "God did not afflict me with right judgment." (v.6)
